title: Device Online/Offline Status Monitoring (Meraki)
dataSources: `sourcetype=meraki:api`; Meraki API (`meraki:api` etc.) and optional organization syslog/HTTPS
spl:
index=cisco_network sourcetype="meraki:api"
| stats latest(status) as device_status, latest(last_status_change) as status_change_time, count(eval(status="offline")) as offline_count by network_id
| eval offline_pct=round(offline_count*100/count, 2)
| where offline_count > 0
description: Tracks device connectivity status to quickly identify and respond to device failures.
value: Tracks device connectivity status to quickly identify and respond to device failures.
implementation: Poll devices API for status. Alert on offline devices.
visualization: Device status table; offline count gauge; status change timeline.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: `Cisco Meraki Add-on for Splunk` (Splunkbase 5580).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `sourcetype=meraki:api`.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Poll devices API for status. Alert on offline devices.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=cisco_network sourcetype="meraki:api"
| stats latest(status) as device_status, latest(last_status_change) as status_change_time, count(eval(status="offline")) as offline_count by network_id
| eval offline_pct=round(offline_count*100/count, 2)
| where offline_count > 0
```

Understanding this SPL

**Device Online/Offline Status Monitoring (Meraki)** — Tracks device connectivity status to quickly identify and respond to device failures.

Documented **Data sources**: `sourcetype=meraki:api`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): `Cisco Meraki Add-on for Splunk` (Splunkbase 5580). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: cisco_network; **sourcetype**: meraki:api. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=cisco_network, sourcetype="meraki:api". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by network_id** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **offline_pct** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• Filters the current rows with `where offline_count > 0` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.


Step 3 — Validate
In Meraki Dashboard, open the same organization or network, compare the metric (status, event feed, or admin log) to the Splunk result, and confirm the TA’s API key, org ID, and optional syslog reach the same index and sourcetype you used in the search.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Device status table; offline count gauge; status change timeline.
grandmaExplanation: We help you know quickly when a Meraki box drops offline, before users open tickets about dead Wi‑Fi or VPN.
knownFalsePositives: Brief cellular or power blips to appliances can flip offline/online; use duration filters and local ping where possible before heavy paging.
